contributions.title: feat: migrate WeTTY server to Rust (axum + tower + socketioxide) with napi-rs Node.js bridge
contributions.bodyHTML:
Replaces the Express/Socket.IO server with a Rust crate while keeping the TypeScript browser client and CLI entry point untouched. A napi-rs native addon maintains a backward-compatible start() / decorateServerWithSsh() API. mise manages the mixed Rust + Node.js toolchain.

Rust crate (crates/wetty-server/)

  • app.rs — axum Router with tower middleware: SecurityLayer (CSP/X-Frame-Options), CompressionLayer, ServeDir, TraceLayer, prometheus /metrics route
  • socket.rs — socketioxide connection handler wiring PTY lifecycle to Socket.IO events
  • pty.rs — portable-pty spawner bridged to tokio channels; uses rt.enter() so tokio::spawn works correctly from OS threads
  • flowcontrol.rs — port of TinyBuffer (2 ms / 512 KiB flush) + FlowControlServer low/high watermark
  • command.rs — SSH command builder mirroring src/server/command/
  • ssl.rs — rustls TLS config (ring backend); supports cert/key path and Unix socket binding
  • lib.rs — napi bindings behind optional node-binding feature so cargo test links without Node.js

TypeScript shim

src/server.ts becomes a thin wrapper — same exported signatures, delegates to the .node addon:

import addon from '../build/wetty_server.node';
export const start: typeof addon.start = addon.start;
export const decorateServerWithSsh: typeof addon.decorateServerWithSsh = addon.decorateServerWithSsh;

Breaking: return type changes from Promise<SocketIO.Server> to Promise<ServerHandle>. Callers that interact with the returned server instance will need to adapt.

Toolchain & build

  • .mise.toml — pins Node 22, Rust 1.96.0, pnpm 9.4.0; defines build, test, lint, check:rust tasks
  • scripts/copy-addon.sh — single source of truth for copying .so/.dylib/.dll → build/wetty_server.node; referenced by mise, build.js, and the build:rust npm script
  • CI — replaced actions/setup-node + pnpm/action-setup with jdx/mise-action@v2; added cargo test step

Tests

  • 48 Rust unit tests across all modules
  • 10 HTTP integration tests (tests/app_test.rs) — routes, security headers, redirect behaviour
  • 3 TLS integration tests (tests/ssl_test.rs)
  • src/server.spec.ts — TypeScript smoke tests for the shim

contributions.title: Harden disconnect overlay by rendering reason as plain text
contributions.bodyHTML:
The disconnect overlay still used innerHTML for the server-provided reason string, leaving one remaining HTML-injection surface after prior client-side XSS hardening. This change aligns the disconnect path with the same safe rendering approach already used elsewhere.

  • Problem scope

    • src/client/wetty/disconnect.ts updated the #msg node with raw HTML-capable assignment for disconnect reasons.

  • Change made

    • Replaced HTML interpretation with text-only rendering in disconnect(reason?: string).
    • Preserved existing fallback behavior ('Session ended') and control flow.

  • Resulting behavior

    • Disconnect reasons are displayed exactly as text content, not parsed markup.
const msg = document.getElementById('msg');
if (msg !== null) msg.textContent = reason ?? 'Session ended';

contributions.title: fix: Docker CLI flag forwarding in runtime image
contributions.bodyHTML:
Recent wettyoss/wetty Docker images (notably v3.0.2) fail on startup when flags are passed via docker run/Compose (node: bad option: --ssh-host...). The container command was being interpreted by Node’s default entrypoint instead of WeTTY’s CLI.

  • Root cause

    • Docker runtime inherited the Node base image entrypoint, so CLI flags provided to the container were treated as Node runtime flags.

  • Change

    • Updated the WeTTY runtime image startup to execute WeTTY directly as container entrypoint.
    • This makes container arguments flow to build/main.js (yargs parser) as intended.

  • Behavior impact

    • Restores support for flags passed in command/container args (e.g. --ssh-host, --ssh-port) without restart loops.
- CMD ["pnpm", "start"]
+ ENTRYPOINT ["node", "./build/main.js"]

contributions.title: docs: align configuration docs with current runtime defaults
contributions.bodyHTML:
The docs still described /wetty-based defaults, while runtime defaults now serve from /. This updates user-facing and API docs so defaults, examples, and proxy guidance match current behavior.

  • Default base path and URL examples

    • Updated docs that implied /wetty as default to /.
    • Rewrote example URLs to use root-based defaults (http://host:3000, /ssh/<username>).

  • API defaults accuracy

    • Corrected serverConf.base default to '/'.
    • Corrected serverConf.title default to 'WeTTY - The Web Terminal Emulator'.

  • Proxy docs clarity

    • Clarified that /wetty is a non-default prefixed base path used when explicitly configured (for nginx/apache setups), not the built-in default.
- | [serverConf.base]  | `string` | `'/wetty/'` |
+ | [serverConf.base]  | `string` | `'/'`       |

- http://yourserver:3000/wetty/ssh/<username>
+ http://yourserver:3000/ssh/<username>

contributions.title: fix: Potential fix for code scanning alert no. 6: Server-side URL redirect
contributions.bodyHTML:
Potential fix for https://github.com/butlerx/wetty/security/code-scanning/6

General fix: ensure redirect targets are validated/normalized to a strict local-path form before calling res.redirect, and fall back to a safe default if validation fails.

Best fix here (minimal behavior change): in src/server/socketServer/middleware.ts, update redirect(...) so it:

  1. Computes the same canonicalized target as today.
  2. Validates it is a relative local URL path (starts with /, not //, no scheme like http:).
  3. Redirects only if valid; otherwise redirects to /.

This preserves existing trailing-slash behavior for legitimate paths while adding explicit safety checks that satisfy the CodeQL finding.

Suggested fixes powered by Copilot Autofix. Review carefully before merging.

contributions.title: Add Support for using a pipe rather then a socket
contributions.bodyHTML:
We need to add support for using a pipe to send metrics rather then a socket between processes.

I couldnt find a nice way to reuse the other interface as the model for communication is to different

contributions.title: Expose runtime metrics
contributions.bodyHTML:
Addresses #610

  • Add an IPC metric reporter and collector
    • uses the interprocess crate to get cross platform support
  • Added prometheus endpoint for exposing metrics
  • uses the tokio-metrics crate to expose tokio metric
  • Added the Following metrics
    • granian_number_workers
    • granian_worker_started_total
    • granian_worker_stopped_total
    • granian_requests_processed_total
    • granian_python_call_latency
    • granian_http_responses_total
    • granian_requests_processed_total

Note: I'm not familiar enough with granian to know how to capture all the metrics listed in the issue, so pointers on where to collect those metrics from would be appreciated

Example of prom endpoint

# TYPE granian_total_busy_duration counter
granian_total_busy_duration{worker_id="2"} 0
granian_total_busy_duration{worker_id="1"} 0

# HELP granian_min_busy_duration The minimum amount of time a worker thread was busy
# TYPE granian_min_busy_duration counter
granian_min_busy_duration{worker_id="2"} 0
granian_min_busy_duration{worker_id="1"} 0

# HELP granian_connections_total Total number of connections established by each worker since startup
# TYPE granian_connections_total counter
granian_connections_total{worker_id="2"} 1

# HELP granian_requests_processed_total Total count of HTTP requests processed by each worker since startup
# TYPE granian_requests_processed_total counter
granian_requests_processed_total{method="GET",path="/",worker_id="2"} 3

# HELP granian_http_responses_total Counter of HTTP responses grouped by status code per worker
# TYPE granian_http_responses_total counter
granian_http_responses_total{status_code="2xx",worker_id="2"} 3

# HELP granian_max_busy_duration The maximum amount of time a worker thread was busy
# TYPE granian_max_busy_duration counter
granian_max_busy_duration{worker_id="1"} 0
granian_max_busy_duration{worker_id="2"} 0

# HELP granian_worker_started_total Total number of workers started since the application started
# TYPE granian_worker_started_total counter
granian_worker_started_total 2

# HELP granian_number_workers Current number of active workers in the Granian application
# TYPE granian_number_workers gauge
granian_number_workers 2

# HELP granian_global_queue_depth The number of tasks currently scheduled in the runtime's global queue
# TYPE granian_global_queue_depth gauge
granian_global_queue_depth{worker_id="2"} 0
granian_global_queue_depth{worker_id="1"} 0

# HELP granian_min_park_count The minimum number of times any worker thread parked
# TYPE granian_min_park_count gauge
granian_min_park_count{worker_id="1"} 0
granian_min_park_count{worker_id="2"} 0

# HELP granian_total_park_count The number of times worker threads parked
# TYPE granian_total_park_count gauge
granian_total_park_count{worker_id="1"} 0
granian_total_park_count{worker_id="2"} 0

# HELP granian_max_park_count The number of times worker threads parked
# TYPE granian_max_park_count gauge
granian_max_park_count{worker_id="1"} 0
granian_max_park_count{worker_id="2"} 0

# HELP granian_workers_count The number of worker threads used by the runtime
# TYPE granian_workers_count gauge
granian_workers_count{worker_id="1"} 1
granian_workers_count{worker_id="2"} 1

# HELP granian_python_call_latency Time spent executing Python application code within each worker, measured in seconds
# TYPE granian_python_call_latency summary
granian_python_call_latency{status="200",worker_id="2",quantile="0"} 0.000135375
granian_python_call_latency{status="200",worker_id="2",quantile="0.5"} 0.00014811818813720976
granian_python_call_latency{status="200",worker_id="2",quantile="0.9"} 0.00014811818813720976
granian_python_call_latency{status="200",worker_id="2",quantile="0.95"} 0.00014811818813720976
granian_python_call_latency{status="200",worker_id="2",quantile="0.99"} 0.00014811818813720976
granian_python_call_latency{status="200",worker_id="2",quantile="0.999"} 0.00014811818813720976
granian_python_call_latency{status="200",worker_id="2",quantile="1"} 0.000391375
granian_python_call_latency_sum{status="200",worker_id="2"} 0.000674875
granian_python_call_latency_count{status="200",worker_id="2"} 3
